subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Logical progression of ideas (hypothesis â scatterplot â regression â Pearson r â residuals)
Headings, table of contents and numbered sections facilitate navigation
weaknesses: Occasional digressions (lengthy poverty definition, personal anecdote) interrupt flow
Some figures failed to render and formulas are repeated unnecessarily
Minor misalignments between table of contents and section titles reduce polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and well organized, with a clear logical development and sound structure. However, interruptions in focus (anecdotal passages), redundancy in formulas and missing figures prevent it from reaching the highest band. A score of 3 reflects strong organization and clarity with room for improved conciseness and polish.

strengths: Use of standard statistical symbols and definitions for key variables
Effective deployment of multiple representations (tables, scatterplot, residual plot, ANOVA output)
Interpretations of graphical outputs are generally well articulated
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation (e.g. mixing LaTeX/plain text, misâwritten mean formula, conflicting subscripts)
Errors and contradictions in the reported correlation values
Missing figure numbers and ambiguous references reduce clarity and consistency
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work employs appropriate mathematical language and multiple representations effectively, but inconsistent notation and calculation inconsistencies undermine clarity. These shortcomings limit the band to 2 despite solid interpretative commentary.

strengths: Personal anecdote provides genuine context and initial engagement with the topic
weaknesses: Limited evidence of independent mathematical decision-making beyond textbook procedures
Approach follows standard recipes with little novel insight
Minimal creativity or initiative in extending or adapting methods
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the topic choice and introductory anecdote demonstrate personal engagement, the exploration lacks sustained independent thinking or creative mathematical initiative, meriting only the lowest non-zero band (1/3).

strengths: Acknowledges data reliability issues and potential bias in crime reporting
Makes a basic connection between residuals and underestimation of variables
weaknesses: Reflection is brief and confined to a single evaluation paragraph
Lacks critical analysis of model assumptions (linearity, normality)
No deep critique of statistical choices (e.g., ANOVA grouping, significance thresholds)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The reflection shows some awareness of data limitations and bias but remains superficial, with limited critical depth and few sustained connections to the explorationâs mathematical understanding, warranting a band-1 score.

strengths: Appropriate use of course-level mathematics: correlation, linear regression, residuals and ANOVA
Conceptual explanations of r, rÂ², residuals and ANOVA output demonstrate good grasp
Calculations are largely plausible and address the research question directly
weaknesses: Computational errors (mis-written formulas, sample r calculation slip) and misapplication of ANOVA
Lack of worked examples for key formulas and improper grouping for ANOVA test
Insufficient sophistication and rigor in justifying methods and interpreting effect sizes
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student applies relevant AI SL mathematics competently and explains concepts clearly, but computational mistakes and methodological oversights (ANOVA grouping, missing worked examples) limit the work to mid-band performance. A score of 3/6 reflects competent application with notable areas for improvement.

strandSelections.reasoning: Apart from choosing to compare crime and poverty and compiling data, little independent mathematical decision-making is shown; procedures strictly follow textbook recipes. Evidence of original insight into model validity is limited.

The student explains what r, rÂ², residuals and ANOVA output mean, indicating good conceptual grasp, but the justification for selecting ANOVA for two variables and discussion of model assumptions are thin, preventing a judgement of âthorough understanding.â

Most calculations quoted are plausible; however, the worked five-country example produces râ0.01, revealing a computational slip, and the mean of y is mis-written. Such errors mean mathematics is âmostly correctâ rather than fully correct.

At roughly 20 pages the paper contains noticeable redundancy (duplicate formulae for r, lengthy quotations on poverty, repeated explanations of residuals). These passages reduce conciseness, though most content is still germane to the research question, meriting the âmostly relevantâ band.

Standard statistical symbols (r, Å·, s_x) are used and variables are defined, but notation is occasionally incorrect (mean of y written as 1/y, confused sub-scripts). LaTeX and plain text are mixed, creating inconsistency. Hence use is âsome appropriateâ rather than âmostly appropriateâ.

Explanations are generally understandable, yet some passages are ambiguous (mean formula error, contradiction between r=0.01 sample and r=0.523 overall). Such inconsistencies lower clarity, placing the work in the âmostly clear with some consistencyâ band.

Correlation, linear regression, residuals and one-way ANOVA are all within the AI SL syllabus and directly address the research question, representing appropriate course-level mathematics used in context. Usage is competent but not especially sophisticated.

The student employs tables, a scatterplot, a residual plot and refers to an ANOVA output. These multiple representations support the argument and are interpreted in text (e.g., positive trend, clustering). Missing image placeholders stop short of âsophisticatedâ, but overall use is effective.
Reflection is largely confined to a brief evaluation paragraph noting data reliability, bias and underestimation. This shows limited but real reflection, falling short of âmeaningful throughoutâ.

Using several standard statistical tests shows a degree of initiative, yet the analyses are directly lifted from on-line calculators and tutorials. Novel or creative mathematical treatment is minimal, justifying the lowest non-zero band.

The investigation follows a recognisable introductionâmethodologyâanalysisâevaluationâconclusion sequence. Ideas build on each other in a generally logical way (e.g., hypothesis â scatterplot â regression â Pearson r â residuals), so the reader can follow the mathematical argument without major jumps. Nevertheless, some steps are only partially justified (choice of ANOVA, treatment of units), and occasional digressions (lengthy poverty definition, autobiographical anecdote) interrupt the flow, preventing the âexcellentâ descriptor.

The student comments that residuals indicate underestimation and that differing crime-reporting methods may cause bias, but does not probe the assumptions of linearity or justify ANOVA groupings. Analysis is therefore basic with limited critique.

The evaluation links findings to a moderate correlation and acknowledges other factors, which shows a partial connection to mathematical understanding, yet depth is shallow and not sustained across the exploration.

Headings, a table of contents and numbered sections give the work clear structure. Tables and appendices are referenced, and each section largely fulfils its stated purpose. However, some figures did not render, and formulas are repeated unnecessarily, so organisation is solid rather than seamless.

The narrative begins with a personal anecdote and the student selects the topic, but thereafter the approach is conventional. The investigation does not demonstrate a clearly sustained personal line of inquiry, so only âsome evidenceâ is present.

annotations.comment: The research question is clear but could be more focused by specifying measurement definitions (e.g., crime index vs crime rate) and ensuring consistency in terminology.

The introduction paragraph is overly lengthy and combines anecdote with general definitions, reducing conciseness. Consider splitting personal narrative and poverty definition into separate, concise sections.
The personal anecdote demonstrating why this topic matters shows genuine engagement and provides a strong personal context for the exploration.
Claiming a simple random sample by âasking DeepSeek AIâ lacks justification. You should explain the sampling algorithm or use a reproducible random method to ensure validity.
The explanation of the scatter plotâs axes and its role in visualising the relationship is well articulated, supporting effective use of this representation.
The formula for the mean of the dependent variable is miswritten as $$\frac{1}{y}=\frac{\Sigma y_i}{n}$$. It should be $$\overline{y}=\frac{\sum y_i}{n}\,$$ for correct notation.
The repeated presentation of mean formulas for x and y adds redundancy. Consider reducing repetition to improve conciseness and maintain flow.

The calculation of Pearsonâs r for the 5-country sample omits the square root in the denominator. Ensure you compute $$r=\frac{\sum(x_i-\bar x)(y_i-\bar y)}{\sqrt{\sum(x_i-\bar x)^2\sum(y_i-\bar y)^2}}$$ accurately.
Only using a 5-country subset without justification for representativeness limits insight. How does this sample relate to the full dataset of 50?
The slope formula uses notation $$s_v$$ for the SD of y but elsewhere uses $$s_y$$. Maintain consistent symbols (e.g., always $$s_x, s_y$$) to avoid confusion.
The interpretation linking a positive slope to a positive relationship is correct, but referencing computed values (e.g., slope, r) alongside the visual trend would strengthen the argument.
The correlation formula reuses âxâ instead of the mean symbol $$\overline{x}$$ in one term; correct this to maintain clarity and consistency.
The calculated sample r (â0.0103) contradicts the overall r (â0.523). Highlight this discrepancy and explain why the 5-country subset is misleading.
The average residual is reported as 8.18 for all 50 countries but the sampleâs average (43.89) appears misaligned; clarify calculation methods and units for residuals.
Applying one-way ANOVA to two continuous variables without grouping into categories is inappropriate; justify categorical grouping or choose a suitable test (e.g., t-test or regression).
The residual plot interpretation mentions only even distribution; include discussion of non-linearity, outliers or heteroscedasticity to demonstrate deeper analysis.
The ANOVA formula graphic is included but no worked example accompanies it. Show at least one calculation using your dataset to demonstrate understanding.
The evaluation section notes data source reliability but omits discussion of linear model assumptions (linearity, independence, normality). Reflect critically on these limitations.
The conclusion restates statistical values but overstates significance without confidence intervals or effect-size context. Consider nuance when interpreting moderate correlations.
The table of contents lists headings that do not exactly match section titles (e.g., âPages 4â vs âScatter Plot Diagramâ formatting). Align headings and page numbers for clarity.
Figure 1âs caption is split into two headings. Combine the title and subtitle into a single caption to improve presentation consistency.
The placeholder for the residual plot image did not render. Ensure all figures are displayed and referenced correctly to maintain structure and coherence.
Section 3.2 introduces Pearsonâs correlation without a figure number. Label each mathematical representation (e.g., Fig. 2) to bolster mathematical communication.
The ANOVA results are accepted without questioning the p-value threshold or exploring what a borderline case might imply. Add critical reflection on significance levels and context.
